full text: Heavy metal contamination of the ecosystem remains one of the severe global threats. Certain microbes and plants have evolved detoxifying pathways to fight the harmful effects of these inorganic metals, paving the door for bioremediation. This review highlights the potential of microbes on remediation of heavy metals in the context of environmental protection and also focuses on the critical tolerance mechanisms used by these microbes in combating heavy metal contaminations. The bioremediation potential of bacteria, fungus, algae, plants, biosurfactants, biofilms and genetically altered microorganisms for the removal of these heavy metals was reviewed in this study. Heavy metals including boron, cobalt, copper, iron, molybdenum, manganese, and zinc are used by microalgae as trace elements for enzymatic processes and cellular metabolism, however other heavy metals like As, Cd, Cr, Pb, and Hg are poisonous to microalgae. Microalgae have reactive groups with active binding sites that can form complexes with contaminants in wastewater, in addition to heavy metals. A two-stage mechanism is used by microalgae to remove heavy metals. The rapid extracellular passive adsorption is the first stage, while the slow intracellular positive diffusion and accumulation is the second. The cell wall of microalgae is primarily composed of polysaccharides (cellulose and alginate), lipids, and organic proteins, and provides many functional groups (such as amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, imidazole, phosphate, sulfonate, thiol, and others) capable of binding heavy metals in addition to cell polymeric substances such as peptides and EPS with uronic groups. Transcriptomic analysis of several microalgal strains revealed the increased expression of transporter genes related to the uptake of specific heavy metal ions and divalent cations. Microalgal species employ heavy metal immobilization, gene regulation, exclusion, and chelation, as well as antioxidants or reducing enzymes that reduce heavy metals via redox processes, to defend themselves against heavy metal toxicity. Microalgae creates protein-heavy metal complexes in their cells without affecting their own function. Heavy metals also stimulate the production of phytochelatins (PCs), which are thiol-rich peptides that interact with heavy metals to reduce their effects. Microalgae produce antioxidant enzymes such as ascorbate peroxidase, catalase, glutathione reductase, peroxidase, and superoxide dismutase (SOD) to combat free radicals released by heavy metals during adsorption, as well as non-enzymatic antioxidants such as carotenoids, cysteine, ascorbic acid, glutathione and proline. Table 3 represents the removal efficiencies obtained for different algal strains.

full text: Biosorption is the ability of biological materials to accumulate ions, atoms or molecules from wastewater or aqueous solutions through physico-chemical pathways of uptake. Algae are photo autotrophs that belong to a wide and diverse eukaryotic organism. The use of algal biomass as biosorbent is gaining popularity as an attractive alternative as they are eco friendly, low cost, have high efficiency in dilute effluents, high surface area to volume, have regeneration and metal recovery potential. Algae can be single cellular organisms as well as from large thallus (large aggregate of single cells). Most algae are microorganisms but macroscopic species occur as see weeds. Phosphate, sulphate, imidazole, carboxyl, amine, sulfhydryl and hydroxyl are some of the metal binding sites of algal cell components. The use of several algae in biosorption has been investigated. Dried biomass of a green alga, Chlorella vulgaris was used in the biosorption of Pb in a single staged batch reactor in the concentration range of 25 -200 mg/L. De Carvalho et al [80] investigated the multi metal sorption with brown marine alga, Ascophyllum nodosum. full text: The paper discusses the impact of heavy metals on fish in Egypt, specifically in the Kafr Elsheikh Governorate, and the potential use of microalgae (Cyanobacteria) to mitigate these effects. It highlights the contamination of agricultural drainage water with heavy metals and other pollutants, which are used in fish farms. The paper mentions the bioaccumulation of heavy metals in fish tissues and the potential for microalgae to act as biosorbents. Specific microalgae like Spirulina and Chlorella are discussed for their antioxidant properties and ability to remove heavy metals from water. The paper suggests the use of microalgae as feed additives to reduce the negative impact of heavy metals on fish.

full text: As the development and utilization of heavy metals in human society more and more, along with the increasing number of heavy metals are released into the water, more and more of the heavy metals are discharged into water bodies. When microalgae are on the stress of heavy metals, it will produce a series of physiological responses to response to environmental stress by itself. Therefore it also has a detoxification mechanism in a certain concentration range, which has great significance for sewage treatment using microalgae. Microalgae can make their own heavy metal concentration be maintained within a certain range of levels by absorption, accumulation, transfer, discharge of the heavy metals. The toxic effects of heavy metals on microalgae is mainly reflected in the way of hindering cell division, inhibiting the photosynthesis as well as the synthesis of organic compounds, reducing the activity of enzyme, etc. The microalgae have a series of detoxification to maintain their growth in water with heavy metal. Algae's detoxification will play a huge role in the water treatment process using algae. The role of the cell surface. Some of the functional groups on the cell wall surface can adsorb 80% of the heavy metals. The detoxification pathway of metallothionein. Phytochelatins( PCs). Phytochelatinis induced by heavy metal ion, and enzymatic synthesis of small peptides, which is rich in cysteine. PCs can be used to maintain the metal ion homeostasis by organisms. The detoxification pathway of cellular enzymes. Vacuolar compartmentalization plays an important role in the process of higher plants' detoxification to heavy metals. Complex of heavy metals-PCs formed in the process of detoxification plays an important role. Once the algae accumulation of heavy metals, it is easy to zoom through the food chain, and ultimately affects human health. When the algae exposed to heavy metal in the water, it can continue to grow in a certain concentration range, and adapts to the adverse external environment through its own series of physiological changes, which has great significance in the use of algae.

full text: In this literature review, the removal of heavy metals from aqueous media by an environmentally friendly method, known as biosorption has been discussed. Biosorption can be referred to as an alternative to the common unsustainable industrial methods currently used. Removal of heavy metals from aqueous media by biosorption can take place by the aid of different types of biomass including algae, fungi, bacteria and plants. The mechanism(s) of biosorption can vary accordingly, mechanisms include physical adsorption, ion exchange, complexation, precipitation and transport across the cells. The efficiency of removal of heavy metals by a specific biosorbent at specified conditions can be compared by the calculated biosorption capacity of the respected biosorbent. Several factors can influence the biosorption capacity of different biosorbents, that mainly includes water pH, temperature, contact time, biomass dosage and initial heavy metal concentration. This literature review focuses on the types of biosorbents, mechanisms of biosorption and factors affecting biosorption capacity. Biosorption is a biological physico-chemical process in which a biological material, such as plant biomass or microorganisms, is used to absorb or adsorb a target species, such as metal ions or dyes. Algae, bacteria, fungi and plants are the most common biosorbents used for the removal of heavy metals from aqueous media. The main biosorption site of the biological sorbents is their cell wall. The cell walls of different biosorbents contain a range of different functional groups that contribute in biosorption process. The potential of biosorption of a metal ion by a biosorbent is evaluated by the biosorption capacity value and equilibrium time for the biosorption process. The higher the biosorption capacity, the higher the amount of heavy metal ion(s) a biosorbent can biosorb. Out of all types of algae and other biosorbents, brown algae has shown the most significant biosorption capacities towards different metal ions. A research evaluated the removal of cadmium(II) and lead(II) from aqueous solution by blue green alga Anabaena sphaerica. The results suggested that this alga has a relatively high biosorption capacities for the removal of cadmium(II) and lead(II), calculated to be 111.1 mg/g and 121.95 mg/g, respectively. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis of algal surface functional groups, suggested that the amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups were responsible for biosorption of those ions. Bacteria are classified as gram-positive bacteria or gram-negative bacteria, the most significant difference between these two types is in their cell wall composition and thickness. Functional groups containing oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur or phosphorus are the common contributors of heavy metal biosorption in bacteria. Fungi started to receive more attention as a biosorbent recently due to the rich cell wall content, which suggests the presence of a wide range functional groups which be involved in metal removal. Plants, as agricultural waste materials and food industries discarded material have been used as biosorbents, it is a form of reusing and recycling those waste materials thus no significant costs are associated with using plant materials. The potential of plant biosorbents are mainly due to the presence of carboxylic and phenolic functional groups in the cellulosic matrix or components associated with cellulose such as lignin and hemicellulose. Lead is a heavy transition metal. Lead(II) ions have high affinity to thio, oxo and phosphate groups that are present in some enzymes and biomolecules in living organisms. Chromium, a toxic, carcinogenic, mutagenic and teratogenic heavy metal, which usually exists as trivalent and hexavalent forms in aqueous environment. Cadmium accumulates in photosynthetic plants and fish, which can then be easily transferred to humans. Cadmium has the potential to bind with cysteine, glutamate, histidine and aspartate ligands in the human body causing iron deficiency. The polluting sources of cadmium are electroplating, smelting, paint pigments, batteries, fertilizers, mining and alloy industries. Biosorption is economically feasible, easy to operate and use, and shows good adsorption efficacy. Biosorption methods can be an alternative to the common industrial methods of removal of heavy metals due to being environmentally friendly and sustainable for use. A major limitation of the researches conducted in this area, studying the biosorption capacity of different biomass, is that they did not encounter for the complexity of real-life contaminated water.

full text: Algae are the main organisms that take up and store heavy metals. Microalgae are used for the bioremediation of wastewater, which does not produce secondary pollution and enables efficient recycling of nutrients. Macroalgae and microalgae have special properties for reducing environmental contamination. The biosorption capacity of green algae species like Spirogyra spp. and Cladophora spp. for lead and copper has been evaluated. Algae are an effective alternative for bioremediation of heavy metals like cadmium and lead. Microalgae can be used to treat domestic wastewater and remove metals and nutrients efficiently. They are a source of peptides that bind heavy metals, with affinities for Ni, Co, Cd, and Cu. The tables provide detailed information on seaweed and microalgae applications for metal biosorption.

full text: Bioremoval, the use of biological systems for the removal of metal ions from polluted waters, has the potential to achieve greater performance at lower cost than conventional wastewater treatment technologies for metal removal. Microalgae are discovered to be a useful organism in many applications, like food, dietary supplements, medicine, fuel, and waste treatment. Serious metal pollution could be a problem of nice environmental concern. Some micro-organism strains are best-known that show very strong activity affinity for Hg, Au, Cu, Zn, Pb, and metal. Some algae can take up serious metal ions and organic pollutants from the contaminated waters. The buildup of metals by algae, bacteria, fungi and yeast has been extensively studied inside the last twenty years of the organism studied, algae are gaining increasing attention, thanks to the particular incontrovertible fact that algae, considerably marine algae, are an upscale provide within the oceanic atmosphere, relatively low price to technique and able to accumulate high metal content. The activity on the cell surface is that the dominant mechanism every surface activity and internal diffusion are involved inside the uptake of metals by algae. Metal can cause to a lower place short exposure: abdominal pain, diarrhea, vomiting, and yellow skin, but to a lower place long-term exposure, its cause: internal organ injury, yellow eyes, aluminous vogue in mouth, weakness. Serious metal process takes place in a very try of phases: 1) a speedy binding of ions to the skin of the cell wall, and 2) a slow transfer of metal particle into the algal cell. The algal species used in this study were isolated from Eastern harbor of Mediterranean Sea coast of Alexandria, Egypt. Copper stock solutions were prepared from copper sulfate (CuSO 4 .5H 2 O). Algal tolerance to different copper concentrations (5, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mg/L) was achieved by the determination of algal growth as a chlorophyll a. The results of the present study regarding the effect of contact time on copper removal by P. formosum and O. simplicissima are depicted in Figure 4. By increasing the exposure time (to 24h), the percentage of cu 2+ removal decreases gradually till equilibrium establishment between the percentage of cu 2+ removal by algal cells. The effect of temperature on Cu 2+ removal by P. formosum and O. simplicissima is presented Figure 5. The most removal potency (90%) occurred at optimum temperature 30 °C by P. formosum. The effect of pH on Cu 2+ removal using P. formosum and O. simplicissima is demonstrated in Figure 6. The maximum Cu 2+ removal by P. formosum was 90% at pH 7, at equilibrium time to 24h. The present study provides clear evidence that the addition of copper in various concentrations brings about significant differences in the growth of microalgal species due to the differences in their resistances. Copper removal efficiency by P. formosum and O. simplicissima were 90% and 80%, respectively. The amount of adsorption depends on such factors as pH, temperature, initial metal ion concentration, and contact time. Therefore, applying the optimal condition is recommended in order to achieve higher removal efficiency.

full text: The textile industry generates large volumes of wastewater, as an example, ≈7×105 tons of organic dyes/year. Textile wastewater is a complex solution composed by organic dyes, phosphates, nitrates and heavy metals. Biological methods include the application of bacteria, yeasts, fungi and microalgae (or their metabolites) in situ or ex situ. In addition, there are approximately 50,000 species of microalgae present on earth, which represents countless strategies of microalgae study and application. Textile wastewater contains organic dyes, phosphates, nitrates, trace heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Zn, Mn, Cr, Ni, Cu, Fe, Ar), etc. Biological approaches are the most prominent eco-friendly treatment, in which microalgae (Chlorella vulgaris, Chlorella pyrenoidosa, Spirogyra sp., Oscillatoria tenuisin and Scenedesmus sp.) have risen prominence due to their potential to simultaneously textile wastewater bioremediation, CO2 mitigation and produce high added-value molecules. The textile industry generates large volumes of wastewater ≈7×10 5 tons of organic dyes/year. Textile wastewater is composed by organic dyes, phosphates, nitrates, heavy metals, among others that represent environmental hazard. full text: This review article focuses on recent updates on remediation of industrial wastewater (IWW) through microalgae cultivation. Microalgae are photosynthetic microorganisms that capture solar energy and produce biologically important products such as lipids, proteins, and carbohydrates. IWWs often contain higher concentrations of nitrogen, phosphorous, and carbon sources that support microalgal growth. Microalgae absorb excess nutrients from IWW and assimilate CO2, acting as environmental purifiers. They can produce biofuels and bio-based products, making the process economically feasible and ecofriendly. Microalgae capture nitrogen and phosphorus from wastewater into algal biomass, serving as a slow-release organic fertilizer. The characteristics of IWW-produced microalgae depend on the composition of IWW and cultivation conditions. Microalgal biomass is used for biofuels, food, feed, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals. The paper includes tables detailing microalgae cultivation on various industrial wastewaters, showing biomass productivity and nutrient removal.

full text: Phycoremediation using microalgae is a promising method for biological purification and treatment of industrial wastewater, particularly for removing heavy metals and other contaminants. Microalgae like Scenedesmus, Chlorella, and Oscillatoria have been used to treat wastewater from industries such as paper and petrochemical. Phycoremediation is effective in removing heavy metals from acid mine drainage and has potential for resource recovery. It aligns with sustainable development goals and offers a green approach for contaminant removal.

full text:
The intensification of human activities has resulted in pervasive toxic metal pollution in water bodies, thereby endangering ecosystems and human health through bioaccumulation in food chains.
Decontamination technology, recognized for its cost-effectiveness, versatility, and minimal secondary pollution, has garnered considerable attention.
Among the diverse methods, photosynthetic microalgae exhibit considerable potential in the purification of toxic metals.
These microalgae actively remove toxic metals through diverse molecular mechanisms during their life cycle, and continue to purify water via residual adsorption after death.
This review explores the molecular response mechanisms of microalgae under toxic metal stress and the environmental factors affecting their decontamination efficiency.
A comparative analysis of live vs. non-living algal biomass highlights the dynamic remediation advantages of live algae and the rapid treatment potential of non-living algae.
The present review also discusses the sustainability and regeneration potential of microalgae as biosorbents.
Furthermore, it elucidates the potential of microalgae-based toxic metal applications, while also delineating the challenges in the domain of toxic metal decontamination.
微藻(Microalgae)是一类广泛分布的单细胞或简单多细胞形式的光合微生物, 具有结构简单但功能齐全的细胞体系。
微藻不仅表现出对这些污染物的耐受性, 还能通过代谢调控和抗氧化酶作用, 减轻毒性影响实现自我保护, 展现出独特的净化潜力。
环境因素既可以直接影响微藻对有毒金属的去除效率, 也可以通过改变微藻的生长与生理状况间接影响有毒金属的去除效率。
主要因素包括有毒金属离子环境浓度、pH及温度等。
"生长微藻"与"死亡微藻"的有毒金属去除效率差异
生长微藻适合用于处理低浓度有毒金属的地表水或经过预处理的废水。
对于有毒金属浓度较高、pH波动较大的工业废水, 若超过微藻细胞的耐受限度, 且缺乏藻类生长必需的营养物质, 难以维持藻类的生物活性。

full text: The use of biosorbents for the decontamination of industrial effluent by retaining non-biodegradable pollutants has been investigated. This review investigates the use of different natural microbial biomass types containing polysaccharides, proteins, and lipids as biosorbents in free and immobilized forms. Microbial biomass immobilization using polymeric support ensures efficient biosorbents with good mechanical resistance and easy separation ability. The review presents advances related to main polymers like polysaccharides and microbial cells used for biosorbents production, including algal biomass. The biosorption process is based on microbial cell walls with various functional groups that interact with contaminants. Immobilization increases biosorption capacity up to 12fold compared to using polymers alone. Algal biomass uses functional groups like carboxyl, sulphate, amino, hydroxyl, carbonyl, thioester, phosphodiester, amine, and amide for metal binding. The technology offers flexibility for removing metals, dyes, and antibiotics but has limited practical application due to operational complexity. Wasted biomass offers advantages in cost and sustainability. Alginate and chitosan-based biosorbents show the best biosorption capacities. Research should focus on developing cost-effective carriers and large-scale experiments using real wastewaters.

full text:
The application of living microalgae for the phycoremediation of HMs can include both extracellular and/or intracellular bioremediation strategies, Figure 1 summarizes the main pathways involved in the bioremediation and mitigation of HMs.
The extracellular uptake of HMs by living cells of microalgae may occur by biosorption in the cell wall or in the extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) formed by microalgae in response to stress conditions.
The cell wall is the interface between the intracellular compartment and the external environment (Macfie and Welbourn, 2000). It contains multifunctional macromolecules such as lipids, proteins and carbohydrates, which offer on its surface different negatively charged functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, sulfhydryl, sulfate, phosphate, phenol...etc.
The biosorption of HMs occurs mainly in the cell surface. Thus, modifying their composition and their physicochemical properties can improve the interaction with ions of HMs, increasing thereby the biosorption performance.
Adsorption-based processes have a high potential for HMs absorption, but their common weakness is a lack of selectivity for the absorption of target metal from heterogeneous metal ions solutions (Shen et al., 2017).
The advancement of bioengineering of living microorganisms has recently enabled the construction of new biosorbents by adapting the metallosorption properties of the cell surfaces, in order to increase the adsorption selectivity for target metal species (Wang et al., 2021).
Application of genetic engineering strategy for the enhancement of the intra-cellular uptake of HMs encompass the genetic modification of genes encoding for: metals membrane transporters, high-affinity HM binding proteins such as genetically encoded chelators, enzymes that catalyze the reduction of toxic metals via redox transformations, and enzymes that scavenge reactive oxygen species (Rajamani et al., 2007).
Based on the above considerations, we can infer that the strains of green microalgae had multifaceted mechanisms for removing toxic HMs, including extracellular biosorption, EPS-complexation, intracellular bioaccumulation and compartmentalization, enzymatic reduction, biomethylation, and volatilization.

full text: Heavy metals in industrial wastewaters are posing a serious threat to the environment and to human health. Microalgae are increasingly being seen as potential solutions to this problem as they can remove pollutants through biosorption. This review focuses on the cell wall composition and structure of the most commonly used microalgae in heavy metal removal and shows the role of their cell wall in the biosorption process. The variety of functional groups on the surface of the cell wall (such as carboxyl or amino groups) can act as binding sites for the heavy metals, thus removing them from the environment. In this review we will show that, due to the biochemical properties of microalgal cell walls, biosorption via microalgae has the potential to offer a new eco-friendly, efficient, and cost-effective solution to remove heavy metals from wastewater. Chlorella and Scenedesmus are reported to be two of the most widely used algae for heavy metal removal, due to their high biosorption capacities. The cell wall composition of microalgae has only been studied for a very limited number of strains. The functional groups at the outer layer of the algal cell wall interact with the surrounding environment and can act as binding sites where positively charged metal ions interact with negatively charged groups on the cell wall surface. Biosorption can be defined as the rapid and reversible binding of ions from aqueous solutions onto functional groups present on the surface of biomass. The biosorption process can occur in both live or dead cells. Biosorption is just one of many methods used for heavy metal removal. It offers several advantages in comparison to more traditional wastewater treatment techniques like chemical precipitation or coagulation-flocculation. The architecture and composition of Chlorophyta cell walls is very complex yet very important for biotechnological applications. Overall, the removal of heavy metals by microalgal biomass offers an eco-friendly and sustainable process, which could be improved by further knowledge of the cell wall composition and of the basic mechanisms of biosorption.

full text:
Tannery effluent can cause serious environmental pollution due to its high levels of chromium, chemical oxygen demand (COD), biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) and other pollutants like nitrogen, phosphorus, dyes, sulphur etc.
The problem of chromium in tannery effluent is that it is highly toxic and can be hazardous to aquatic life, wildlife, and human health.
The conventional physicochemical methods for removing Cr from wastewater may generate a vast amount of sludge leading to sludge disposal issues.
Biological means of Cr remediation involve plant-based and microorganism-based approaches, including bacteria, fungi, and microalgae.
Microalgal biomass can remove heavy metals, including Cr, in either a living (bioaccumulation) or non-living (biosorption) state.
Among them several algae species, including Chlorella vulgaris, Scenedesmus sp., Consortium of Phormidium sp. and Chlorella sp., Chlorella sorokiniana, and Arthrospira platensis (Spirulina), have found successful in removing Cr in the range of 100–73.5% from tannery wastewater.
Microalgae are microscopic algae that live in saline or freshwater habitats; they are photosynthetic microorganisms that can convert sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to chemical energy.
Microalgal biomass can be used as the bio-adsorbent for removing Cr through biosorption and bioaccumulation.
Algae can remove both Cr 6+ and Cr 3+ from contaminated aqueous environments.
Microalgae have been widely discussed as a means of removing heavy metals from effluents.
Microalgae can also remove other heavy metals, including Pb, Zn, Cu, As, Cd, Al, Au, Ni.
Microalgae are simple to cultivate, are cost-effective, less nutritional requirements, do not produce hazardous sludge, have a net negative charge with a relatively large particular surface area, and perform at low contaminant levels as well as function at a broad pH level to remove heavy metal.
Microalgae have the necessary adsorption capabilities and the potential to be used as adsorbents to remove Cr from industrial wastewater, notably tannery effluent.

full text: Heavy metals (HMs) have emerged as a significant and complex water pollution problem globally. One promising alternative is the phycoremediation process, which employs microalgae to remove HMs from wastewater. This review delves into the capabilities of microalgae in remediating HM-polluted water, examining the various factors and methods that affect the process. Key improvements that can enhance the efficiency of phycoremediation include immobilizing microalgae to increase their stability and longevity, utilizing binary cultures to harness synergistic effects and implementing cellular and genetic modifications to boost HM uptake and resilience. The integration of algorithms and artificial intelligence can optimize phycoremediation processes by predicting and controlling environmental conditions, enhancing the precision and effectiveness of HM removal. This review thoroughly investigates the significant role of microalgae in the removal of HMs from wastewater. Various factors that influence the effectiveness of microalgae in HM removal, including environmental conditions, species selection and operational parameters, are detailed. A key focus of this review is on recent advancements and improvements that enhance the efficiency of microalgae in removing HMs. The review explores the cutting-edge role of artificial intelligence (AI) in optimizing the phycoremediation process. Microalgae are ideal alternatives for the removal of specific HMs since they thrive autotrophically and heterotrophically, possess elevated surface-to-volume ratios, have HM tolerance, are phototaxic, can be easily genetically manipulated and express phytochelatin. Chlorella vulgaris, Chlorella variabilis, Scenedesmus obliquus and Scenedesmus abundans as well as Chlamydomonas reinhardti have proved to be effective in HM remediation. For HM removal from wastewaters, several strategies can be followed to overcome these challenges and improve the HM removal efficiency. Future research endeavors should focus on the optimization of the parameters and the use of innovative technologies to improve efficiency. AI also plays a crucial role in minimizing the time and effort in the detection of HMs present in wastewater as well as screening for the strains and monitoring the process variables.

full text: Biosorption is a relatively new process that has proven very promising in the removal of contaminants from aqueous effluents. Biosorption experiments over Cu(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), Cr(III), and Ni(II) demonstrated that biomass Cu(II) adsorption ranged from 8.09 to 45.9 mg g−1, while Cd(II) and Cr(VI) adsorption ranged from 0.4 to 10.8 mg g−1 and from 1.47 to 119 mg g−1, respectively. Mechanisms involved in the biosorption process include chemisorption, complexation, surface and pore adsorption-complexation, ion exchange, microprecipitation, hydroxide condensation onto the biosurface, and surface adsorption. Chemical modification and spectroscopic studies have shown that cellular components including carboxyl, hydroxyl, sulfate, sulfhydryl, phosphate, amino, amide, imine, and imidazol moieties have metal binding properties and are therefore the functional groups in the biomass. Column studies using support matrices for biomass immobilization such as silica, agar, polyacrilamide, polysulfone, alginates, cellulase, and different cross-linking agents have been performed to improve the biomass adsorption capacity and reusability. In this review, the salient features of plant-derived materials are highlighted as potential phytofiltration sources in the recovery of toxic heavy and precious metals. Biosorption is a rather complex process affected by several factors. Mechanisms such as chemisorption, complexation, adsorption-complexation on surface and pores, ion exchange, microprecipitation, heavy metal hydroxide condensation, as well as surface adsorption, are involved in the biosorption process. Plant biomass and biomass from agricultural by-products and in some cases appropriately modified have shown to have a high capacity for heavy metal adsorption. Toxic heavy metals such as Pb(II), Cd(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), Cr(III), and Cr(VI), as well as some elements from lanthanide and actinides groups have been successfully removed from contaminated aqueous solutions using different agricultural biomasses.

full text:
Industrialization, natural processes, and urbanization have potentially accelerated the pace and the level of heavy metals (HMs) in soil and underground water.
Algae offer an eco-friendly and sustainable alternative for remediation of HMs from polluted water.
The accumulation of HMs by macro and microalgae is advantageous for phycoremediation compared to other approaches that are not economical and not environmentally friendly.
Algae play a crucial role in aquatic ecosystems by constituting the energy base of the food web for all the aquatic fauna.
Algae affect the quality of water, assist in sewage treatment, and generate biomass.
Algae are known to be an excellent renewable energy source due to their fast rate of growth and their capability to be grown in wastewater or waste land.
The buildup of HMs by algae offers various advantages for phytoremediation over other, less cost-effective and environmentally friendly approaches.
Algae possess various characteristics that make them excellent candidates for selective HMs removal, including include the ability to grow heterotrophically and autotrophically, high surface area/volume ratios, tolerance to HMs, phototaxy, and genetic manipulation potential and phytochelatin expression.
Microalgae are microscopic, aquatic organisms that thrive in both marine and freshwater environments, and have a mechanism of photosynthesis that is similar to that of land plants.
The ability of algae to metabolize and adsorb HMs is linked with their high surface-to-volume ratios, efficient metal uptake, storage systems, and the presence of high-affinity metal-binding groups on their cell surfaces.
Mechanisms for removal of HMs include flocculation, absorption, sedimentation, precipitation, cation and anion exchange, microbiological activity, oxidation/reduction, and uptake and complexation.
Removal of HMs by microalgae is performed directly via two major mechanisms; one is metabolism, which is dependent on the uptake of HMs in cells at low concentrations, and the other is biosorption, which is a passive process of adsorption.
Biosorption is a physio-chemical property that results in removal of HMs by covalent or ionic bonding.
The pH of the adsorbing media strongly influences adsorption of metal ion.
Algae minimize damage induced by metals through exclusion and excretion of metals and by synthesizing proteins and other binding compounds such as glutathione (GSH) and metallothioneins (MTs).
Phytochelatins are peptides that bind to metals and are small, having molecular weights in the range from 2 to 10 kDa.
Phytochelatins are synthesized by the constitutive enzyme called phytochelatin synthase.
Genetic engineering can be used to enhance the creation of transgenic organisms that over-express phytochelatins and metallothioneins that can form complexes with HMs and translocate them into vacuoles, and aid phytoaccumulation and hazardous element removal from aquatic habitats.

full text:
- National industrial effluent remediation strategies are largely outdated, which makes these processes costly and at the same time inefficient.
- Among the most worrying compounds are heavy metals, which have bioaccumulative and toxic characteristics to living beings.
- This paper presents a bibliographical review of the current researches that are applying biomass from microalgae in effluent bioremediation, both domestic and industrial (mainly the latter, since its pollution potential is abruptly greater).
- The diversity of these microorganisms is abundant, propitiating the application of them in various conditions.
- Many species of microalgae produce large amounts of lipids, many of which have potential application in the production of biodiesel.
- The term microalgas does not possess taxonomic value, being commonly referred to groups of algal microorganisms whose cellular composition presents photosynthetic pigments capable of performing oxygenic photosynthesis.
- The microalgas are the interest of the present review, constituting a group of microorganisms whose photosynthetic efficiency is largely greater when compared to higher plants, besides being capable of utilizing both organic and inorganic carbon.
- The biosorption is centered on the potential that certain organisms (animals or plants) present in retaining compounds present in aqueous solutions.
- The biosorption is a process that occurs through varied mechanisms, acting in conjunction or not, with direct correlation to the type of metal to be removed, as well as the organism used.
- The biosorption can be treated as an alternative, or form of joint action, in the operations of detoxification of industrial and residential effluents.
- The complexation is centered on the formation of coordination compounds through the donation of electrons to metal ions, being this electron donor called ligand and responsible for promoting a covalent bond with metal cations (or neutral metal atoms) based on their availability in sharing electrons.
- The adsorption is a process in which a certain substance, whether gaseous, liquid, or solid, becomes attached to the surface of a solid adsorbent material.
- The ion exchange is a process in which there occurs an exchange of ions present in an aqueous solution with a relatively reduced concentration for another, with a higher concentration, through an insoluble material (biomass in the context of biosorption).
- Innumerous researches are being conducted with the intent of determining possible applications for microalgae biomass, many of which are returning very promising results, managing to add value to the generated compounds and/or insert them into existing processes.
- One of these applications is in bioremediation, once the biochemical constitution of microalgae makes biosorption processes viable.
- The potential for bioremediation of microalgae over various compounds is summarized in Table 5.
- The capacity for remediation, both of nutrients and heavy metals, was confirmed by numerous studies, being a cheap and often more efficient form compared to traditional remediation methods.
- The fact that there exist numerous species of microalgas, which can occupy diverse ranges of pH, temperature, CO2 dissolved, etc., serves as a stimulus for more research in this area to be concretized.

full text:
- Cyanidiales-Based Bioremediation of Heavy Metals
- Microalgal-based heavy metal removal has been explored for the past few decades and has been seen as an effective, environment-friendly, and inexpensive method compared to conventional treatment technology.
- Cyanidiales that belong to red algae have the potential for remediation of heavy metals as they can withstand and tolerate extreme stresses of heat, acid salts, and heavy metals.
- This review focuses on the algal species belonging to three genera of Cyanidiales: Cyanidioschyzon, Cyanidium, and Galdieria.
- The result is summarized as maximum removal efficiency at the optimum experimental conditions and based on the parameters affecting the metal ion removal efficiency.
- pH, initial metal concentration, initial algal biomass concentration, algal strains, and growth temperature are the major parameters that affect the heavy metal removal efficiency of Cyanidiales.
- Galdieria sulphuraria was found to be the most frequently used species among different species of Cyanidiales.
- Galdieria sulphuraria was used by Minoda et al. for the recovery of rare earth elements: neodymium (Nd), dysprosium (Dy), lanthanum (La), and Cu.
- Galdieria sulphuraria was also used by Ju et al. for metal recovery, which showed a significant recovery of Au, Pd, and Pt from the metal ion solutions.
- The removal efficiency of Cu (II) is greater when using Cyanidiales species.
- The removal efficiency of Cd (II) and Pb (II) is greater when using other algal species.
- pH, IMC, IBC, algal strains, and temperature primarily influence the HM bioremediation efficiency.
- Galdieria sulphuraria is the most frequently used species among the Cyanidiales and can remove almost 100% of HM from the aqueous solution at optimum experimental conditions.

full text:
The general goal of bioremediation technology is to use biological processes to remove contaminants such as pesticides, heavy metals, radionuclides, organic waste, and others from contaminated sites or industrial discharges.
High concentrations of various contaminants are present in industrial effluent that is often dumped into water bodies.
Lead, uranium, selenium, zinc, arsenic, cadmium, chromium, mercury, copper, and nickel are some of the most important contaminants in this regard.
Bioremediation methods are often categorized as in situ i.e., at the contaminated location, or ex situ which refers to removing a pollutant from its original location and treating it somewhere else.
One effective approach is to utilize the beneficial interactions between microbes and plants, utilizing the distinct advantages of each application.
The most important microbial strategies used in bioremediation of heavy metals include biosorption, bioaugmentation, bioaccumulation, biomineralization, microbial transformation, and rhizoremediation.
Heavy metals can be extracted by biosorption from polluted water sources, mining runoff, and industrial effluents.

full text: The paper provides a critical review of biosorption, focusing on its mechanisms, applications, and potential for industrial use. It highlights the use of microbial systems, including microalgae, for the biosorption of metals such as copper and other critical metals from solutions. Despite its potential, industrial application remains limited. The paper discusses various aspects of biosorption, including its comparison with ion exchange technology and its role in environmental biotechnology.

Title: Remediation of Heavy Metals in Polluted Water by Immobilized Algae: Current Applications and Future Perspectives
Authors: Zhonghao Chen, A. Osman, David W. Rooney, Wen-Da Oh, P. Yap
Microalgae Species:
- Specific species/strain names: Chlorella vulgaris, Spirulina platensis, Tetraspora sp. CU2551, Nitzschia palea
- Living or dead biomass: Living biomass

- Source/cultivation conditions: Not mentioned
Target Metals:
- Primary metals of interest: Cu, Ni
- Additional heavy metals: Cd, Pb, Zn, Cr, As
- Radioactive metals: Not mentioned
- Single metal or multi-metal systems: Both single and multi-metal systems were studied
- Initial metal concentrations tested: Concentrations above 100 mg/L for some metals
Wastewater Type: Not mentioned (the paper discusses general concepts and mechanisms related to heavy metal removal by immobilized algae but does not specify details about the type or characteristics of wastewater used in any experiments or case studies)
Biosorption Performance:
- Removal efficiency: 96.4% for Cu(II) over 180 minutes; 91.1% for Cd(II) over three cycles.
- Biosorption capacity: Specific values provided in tables.
- Maximum uptake achieved: Not explicitly mentioned.
- Equilibrium time required: 30 minutes to several hours depending on the metal and conditions.
- Comparison to other treatment methods: Immobilized algae are more efficient and cost-effective.
- Statistical significance of results: Not explicitly mentioned.
Process Conditions:
- pH range tested and optimal pH: pH 5 for Cu ions, pH 6 for Fe(II), Mn(II), and Zn(II)
- Temperature conditions: Optimal at 35°C for Cu(II) removal
- Contact time or residence time: Optimal times range from 180 minutes to 450 minutes
- Biomass concentration or dosage: Higher dosages may reduce effective surface area due to cellular aggregation
- Agitation/mixing conditions: Not explicitly mentioned
- Any process modifications or enhancements tested: Use of different immobilization techniques like adsorption, encapsulation, entrapment, and self-immobilization
Mechanisms & Factors:
- Proposed biosorption mechanisms: ion exchange, complexation, physical adsorption
- Cell wall components involved: proteins, polysaccharides, lipids
- Factors affecting performance: competing ions, pH effects, temperature effects
- Kinetic and equilibrium modeling results: kinetic rate depends on contact time; equilibrium reached when biosorption and desorption rates are equal
- Desorption and regeneration capabilities: biochar pellets maintain 91.1% adsorption efficiency over three cycles; 3.56-4.87% reduction in removal efficiency after five cycles
Study Scale: Laboratory batch experiments; Most research is restricted to the laboratory, with a need for pilot-scale testing for certain processes.
Key Findings:
- Most effective microalgae-metal combinations: Biosorption is effective for removing heavy metals like copper and cadmium.
- Advantages over conventional treatment methods: Cost-effective, efficient, environmentally safe, increased resistance to harmful conditions, reduced cell leakage.
- Economic feasibility or cost considerations: Reduced harvesting costs, potential for metal reuse, but increased costs due to immobilization carriers.

- Recommendations for industrial implementation: Use of hybrid technologies, microbial co-immobilization strategies.
- Future research needs highlighted: Detailed life cycle analyses, field-scale assessments.
full text: The paper discusses the use of immobilized algae for the bioremediation of heavy metals, focusing on optimization strategies and sustainability. It covers various mechanisms of heavy metal removal by immobilized algae, including chelation, ion exchange, and electrostatic interactions. The paper highlights the importance of parameters such as adsorbent content, initial metal ion concentration, temperature, pH, and contact time in affecting biosorption efficiency. It also discusses the role of different carriers in immobilization and their impact on sustainability. Life cycle assessment and economic analysis are emphasized as tools to evaluate the sustainability of immobilized algae technology. The paper includes tables that provide specific data on the immobilization methods, adsorbent dosages, optimal conditions, and maximum adsorption capacities for various heavy metals.

Title: Recent trends in Ni(II) sorption from aqueous solutions using natural materials
Authors: S. Charazińska, E. Burszta-Adamiak, P. Lochyński
Venue: Reviews in Environmental Science and Bio\/technology
Microalgae Species: Not mentioned (the paper does not provide specific details about microalgae species, their treatment, or form used for biosorption)
Target Metals:
- Primary metals of interest: Ni, Cu, Co
- Additional heavy metals: Cd, Pb, Zn, Cr, As
- Radioactive metals: Not mentioned
- Multi-metal systems were studied
- Initial metal concentrations tested: 99 mg/L for bamboo-activated carbon, 32.5 mg/L for regenerated cellulose
Wastewater Type:
- Industrial sector/source: Electroplating, nickel-chromium plating
- Real industrial wastewater: Yes, used in some studies
- Wastewater composition and complexity: Complex, with varying pH and concentrations
- pH, conductivity, and other interfering substances: Not specifically mentioned
- Volume or scale of wastewater treated: Not specified
Biosorption Performance:
- Removal efficiency: Up to 98.7% (bamboo-activated carbon), often exceeding 80%
- Biosorption capacity: Highest values for calcinated eggshells (769 mg Ni·g −1), microbial flocculant GA1 (225 mg Ni·g −1), and Plantanus orientalis bark (286 mg Ni·g −1)
- Maximum uptake: Not explicitly mentioned in the text
- Equilibrium time: Shortest 15 minutes (peat), longest 960 minutes (Meerchaum)
- Comparison to other treatment methods: Limited to laboratory-scale studies; potential for comparison with traditional methods like reverse osmosis and ion exchange
- Statistical significance: Not mentioned in the text
Process Conditions:
- pH range tested: 2.5 to 7.5
- Optimal pH: 5 to 7
- Temperature conditions: Generally exothermic and temperature-independent; some studies showed increased biosorption with increasing temperature
- Contact time: Varied from 15 minutes to 960 minutes
- Biomass concentration/dosage: Specific values mentioned in some studies (e.g., 30 g/L and 10 g/L)
- Agitation/mixing conditions: Not detailed but implied to affect adsorption rates
- Process modifications/enhancements: Acid activation of biosorbents
Mechanisms & Factors:
- Proposed biosorption mechanisms: Chemisorption and ion exchange are the most common mechanisms.
- Cell wall components involved: Various functional groups, likely including proteins and polysaccharides, are involved in biosorption.
- Factors affecting performance: pH (optimal range 7.0-8.0), temperature (especially for living biomass), and competing ions.
- Kinetic and equilibrium modeling results: Pseudosecond-order kinetic model and Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models are commonly used.
- Desorption and regeneration capabilities: Desorption under acidic conditions is effective; recycling and reuse increase economic viability.
Study Scale: Laboratory batch experiments; Duration of study: 5 years; Scale limitations or challenges mentioned: Transition from lab scale to full scale necessary; Cost and time considerations in industrial applications
Key Findings:
- Most effective microalgae-metal combinations: Not specifically mentioned in the context of microalgae; however, calcinated eggshells and Plantanus orientalis bark are highlighted for nickel sorption.
- Advantages over conventional treatment methods: Use of low-cost, abundant natural materials; potential for regeneration and reuse.
- Economic feasibility or cost considerations: Emphasis on cost-effectiveness of modifying biowastes for large-scale use.
- Limitations or challenges identified: Need for industrial-scale verification; challenges related to regeneration and reuse.
- Recommendations for industrial implementation: Consider material availability, price, ease of use, and environmental impact.
- Future research needs highlighted: Search for new waste materials with high sorption capacity; development of cost-effective modification methods.
full text: The paper discusses the use of natural materials for the adsorption of heavy metal ions, specifically focusing on nickel. It reviews studies on biosorbents for nickel removal and classifies them based on their origin. The paper highlights the importance of transferring laboratory experiments to an industrial scale for practical application. It provides a classification system for adsorbents into biomass, composites, waste, peat, and miscellaneous categories. The paper notes that biomass is the most frequently studied category, with plant materials being dominant. Algae and fruits are mentioned as materials used for metal removal, which is relevant to microalgae-based biosorption. The paper discusses the mechanisms of biosorption, including chemisorption and ion exchange, which are relevant to copper and other metals. The tables at the end of the paper provide detailed information on various materials and their sorption capacities, which could be relevant to microalgae-based biosorption.

full text:
Heavy metals are natural constituents of the environment, but indiscriminate use for human purposes has altered their geochemical cycles and biochemical balance.
The role of microorganisms and plants in biotransformation of heavy metals into nontoxic forms is well-documented, and understanding the molecular mechanism of metal accumulation has numerous biotechnological implications for bioremediation of metal-contaminated sites.
The present review investigates the abilities of microorganisms and plants in terms of tolerance and degradation of heavy metals.
Bioremediation is an innovative and promising technology available for removal of heavy metals and recovery of the heavy metals in polluted water and lands.
Microorganisms have developed various strategies for their survival in heavy metal-polluted habitats, these organisms are known to develop and adopt different detoxifying mechanisms such as biosorption, bioaccumulation, biotransformation and biomineralization, which can be exploited for bioremediation either ex situ or in situ.
Microorganisms uptake heavy metals actively (bioaccumulation) and/or passively (adsorption).
Fungi of the genera Penicillium, Aspergillus and Rhizopus have been studied extensively as potential microbial agents for the removal of heavy metals from aqueous solutions.
Phytoremediation basically refers to the use of plants and associated microorganisms to partially or completely remediate selected contaminants from soil, sludge, sediments, wastewater and ground water.
Phytoremediation utilizes a variety of plant processes and the physical characteristics of plants to aid in remediation of contaminated sites.
Genetically engineered microorganisms (GEM) are organisms whose genetic material has been altered using recombinant DNA technology to generate a character-specific efficient strain for bioremediation of soil, water and activated sludge by exhibiting enhanced degrading capabilities against a wide range of chemical contaminants.
Genomics has been explored and used mainly in microbial genetics and in agriculture, e.g., GMO crops, but it is now a new tool for bioremediation.

full text: Industrial heavy metal pollution and other effluents from chemical industries are of great concern for the ecosystem. The physicochemical methods are expensive and these results in the incomplete removal of metal ions. As a result, other alternative methods such as algae bioremediation are taken into account which have the ability to absorb metal ions and this very idea can be used in the treatment of wastewater. In brown algae, the order Laminariales and Fucale species are considered as efficient groups of algae while bio-sorption is considered, because their cell wall is very rich in extracellular polymers and polysaccharides. The sorption capacity of an algae cell surface, to a particular ion depends on the number of functional groups present in the algae cells. In recent years, studying mechanisms of bio-sorption on algal biomass shows that, for either, in separation of metal from industrial effluents or to recover metal from the solution in waste water, brown algal biomass is highly effective with metal during interaction on cell walls. The brown algae are a significantly efficient class of bio sorbents in comparison with other algal species. The implementation of algae bio-sorption technology in industrial and environmental remediation also needs better understanding about its parameters, initial concentrations, contact times and physicochemical method conditions. Successful bio-sorption process involves increasing the biomass productivity of wastewater full grown algae; it is also attractive when combining them with waste water treatment with downstream processing for cultivation. The important advances that emerged in recent years are showing significant improvement when compared with the goals in earlier days.

full text: The paper discusses various bio-based technologies for metal recovery from waste streams, focusing on microbial metabolism-dependent and metabolism-independent mechanisms. It highlights the potential of biosorption, bioaccumulation, and bioelectrochemical systems, as well as the use of metal-binding peptides and siderophores. The paper emphasizes the importance of understanding the fundamental principles behind these technologies to increase selectivity and sensitivity in metal recovery. It addresses the challenges of dealing with complex polymetallic solutions and the need for further research to optimize these technologies. The tables provide specific data on the removal/recovery efficiencies of various metals using different biosorption processes and bioelectrochemical systems. The paper concludes that bio-based recovery technologies offer promising opportunities for treating complex liquid waste streams and selectively recovering metals.

full text: The full text of the paper discusses the effects of metals, metalloids, and metallic nanoparticles on microalgae growth and industrial product biosynthesis. full text: The paper discusses the removal of Ni(II) ions from wastewater using various adsorbents, including algae. Algae are highlighted as a promising biosorbent due to their wide availability, low cost, and high metal sorption ability. Specific types of algae like Dunaliella sp. and Kappaphycus alvarezii are mentioned for their potential in removing heavy metals from wastewater. The paper provides detailed information on the biosorption process, including the use of FT-IR and SEM to study interactions between metal ions and algae surfaces. Isothermal and kinetic models are discussed to understand the adsorption process, with specific data on the sorption capacity and efficiency of different algae species.

full text:
Microalgae-Mediated Biosorption for Effective Heavy Metals Removal from Wastewater: A Review

This review comprehensively examines the role of microalgae in addressing heavy metal pollution, with a primary focus on their effective removal from wastewater. Microalgae offer wastewater purification potential across diverse sources and capitalize on wastewater as a growth matrix, yielding valuable bioproducts, biomaterials, and bioenergy. This study also investigates the application of microalgae in decentralized water treatment systems (DWTSs), where the decentralized nature of these systems proves advantageous in addressing heavy metal contaminants directly at the point of generation or use.

Microalgae play a significant role in wastewater purification, offering advantages over traditional treatments, such as energy efficiency, cost-effectiveness, nutrient recycling, minimal sludge formation, greenhouse gas reduction, and biomass-nutrient recovery. The biosorption process, originating from studies of heavy metals' effects on microbes in fermentation, employs biomass, living or non-living, wet or dry, to cleanse wastewater.

Microalgae have demonstrated their efficacy in treating domestic wastewater, as shown in Table 2. The biosorption process is influenced by various physicochemical factors such as pH, temperature, and redox reactions. The biosorption process is intricately regulated by the microalgal cell wall, where the chemical composition assumes a pivotal role in dictating the efficacy of biosorption.

Microalgae can counter heavy metal exposure's effects by synthesizing antioxidant enzymes, thereby mitigating the harmful impact of free radicals on their cellular structure. The predominant mechanism employed by microalgae for eliminating heavy metals from wastewater is biosorption.

The potential of algae in heavy metal degradation is significant. The integration of microalgae within decentralized water treatment systems represent a noteworthy progression. This review establishes a sturdy groundwork for forthcoming research aimed at unlocking the full potential of microalgae in confronting the urgent global issue of heavy metal contamination in wastewater.

full text:
In recent decades, environmental pollution has emerged as a core issue, around the globe, rendering it of fundamental concern to eco-toxicologists, environmental biologists, eco-chemists, pathologists, and researchers from other fields.
The widespread occurrence of various pollutants including toxic heavy metals and other emerging hazardous contaminants is a serious concern.
Among them, the biosorption of pollutants using naturally inspired sources, e.g., marine algae, has considerable advantages.
Marine algae have been extensively studied due to their natural origin, overall cost-effective ratio, and effectiveness against a broader pollutant range; thus, they are considered a potential alternative to the conventional methods used for environmental decontamination.
The utilization of marine algae as a biosorbent for the removal of numerous potentially toxic elements has also been reviewed.
Biosorption mechanisms and factors affecting biosorption activities are also discussed in this review.
Potentially toxic elements can be removed from wastewater through biosorption from algae, which is an efficient, safe, and more economical method.
Algae are also used for sorption of heavy radionuclides and can recover metal ions such as gold and silver.
Biosorption mechanisms for potentially toxic element removal include ion exchange and complex formation, whereas electrostatic interaction proceeds at the micro-level.
Different mechanisms have been reported for the removal of potentially toxic elements through biosorption.
Biosorption is a passive process that occurs at a faster rate than bioaccumulation.
Adsorption, chelation/complexation, ion exchange, and surface precipitation are different processes reportedly involved in biosorption.
Among them, ion exchange is considered the principle mechanism of biosorption, which occurred through different functional groups present on the surface of biomass.
The mechanism of biosorption usually depends upon the biomass that is going to be used for the removal of potentially toxic elements.
The biosorption ability of algae cell surface is designated by the availability of the binding moiety, i.e., carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, phosphoryl, sulphuryl, sulfate, carbohydrate, imidazole, phosphate, etc.
The presence of the binding site on the algae cell for potentially toxic elements accumulation is analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectroscopy.
Accumulation of the potentially toxic elements on the algae cell depends on various factors such as the number of functional moieties, the approachability of binding sites, binding constants, and the chemical state of the moieties.
Potentially toxic elements are removed from wastewater by algae, mostly through inactive biomass and non-living algae.
Fewer data are available on using live algae for potentially toxic element removal, as toxic elements poison the algae.
The process of biosorption is more complicated in living algae as compared to non-living algae as biosorption occurs in the growth stage and the intracellular consumption of potentially toxic elements mostly takes place at this stage.
Non-living algal biomass includes polymers such as sugars, glycoproteins, cellulose, pectins, etc., which effectively bind and adsorb potentially toxic elements from wastewater.
The cell wall in algae cells is an initial hindrance to the biosorption of potentially toxic elements.
Most of the binding sites present in algae cells are due to polysaccharides and proteins.
Different algal strains have different cell wall compositions and varying capacity of biosorption of potentially toxic elements.
Brown algae have a high affinity for the biosorption of lead because of the presence of alginate in the cell walls and the capacity of biosorption depends on the binding sites available on the alginate.
Several factors such as metal ion concentration, competing for metal ions, temperature, and pH affect the biosorption of potentially toxic elements in addition to available binding sites on the algae cell.
Cadmium is found in nature in the form of deposits with other elements. The toxic metal is discharged in industrial effluents from phosphate fertilizers, plating, stabilizers, and cadmium-nickel alloy batteries.
Biosorption of cadmium using marine algae is a safe, useful, and eco-friendly solution to wastewater treatment.
Chromium is the seventh most prevalent metal on earth and is found in ores with other metals like chromite (FeCr2O3), chrome ochre (Cr2O3), and crocoite (PbCrO3).
Lead is also a toxic metal that can easily accumulate in plants, the human body, and other living organisms.
Biosorption through algae is an innovative technique for the removal of lead from the ecosystem.
Zinc is helpful in various biochemical processes and controls physiological mechanisms in living tissues.
Zinc has been studied for biosorption in the pH range from 5 to 7.5.
P. lanceolatum algae are a potential contender to remove zinc metal up to 118.66 mg g -1.

Microbial-Based Heavy Metal Bioremediation: Toxicity and Eco-Friendly Approaches to Heavy Metal Decontamination

The removal of heavy metals from wastewater and soil can be accomplished using a variety of conventional methods, such as membrane filtration, reverse osmosis, chemical reduction, and adsorption. Conversely, eco-friendly techniques based on microbes have numerous advantages. This review provides a comprehensive overview of biological processes that remove heavy metal ions, both metabolically dependent and metabolically independent.

As a result of their commendable cost-effectiveness and environmentally sustainable nature, an extensive range of techniques and strategies for bioremediation have been identified, including, but not limited to, biosorption, bioreduction, bioaccumulation, mycoremediation, bacterial bioremediation, and phytoremediation. In addition, it has been established through scientific research that living microorganisms, encompassing microalgae as well as bacteria, and plants are significantly efficacious in the role of bioremediation agents.

The surface of bacterial cells is replete with a myriad of functional groups that are intimately involved in the binding of heavy metals, which may occur either to the cell membrane or in a reduced state. The process of remediating heavy metals can be achieved through the utilization of two distinct mechanisms: metabolically independent mechanisms, which entail the use of inorganic, lifeless materials, and metabolically dependent mechanisms, which involve the use of living cells of bacteria, fungi, and algae that are capable of carrying out the necessary metabolic processes required to transform the heavy metal contaminants into less harmful forms.

Bioremediation is a biological technique that is employed to extract heavy metal ions from sites that have been contaminated with heavy metals. In bioremediation, absorption/adsorption, bioaccumulation, biotransformation, and bioleaching are included in the remediation of toxic metal ions. The phenomenon of microorganisms altering the ionic state of heavy metals is a significant process that has far-reaching implications for their solubility, bioavailability, and movement in both soil and aquatic environments.

The mobilization and immobilization of heavy metals is a crucial aspect of microbial remediation, a complex process that involves a multitude of intricate mechanisms including, but not limited to, oxidation-reduction, chelation, modification of the metallic complex, and biomethylation. The efficacy evaluation of a biosorbent is contingent upon the array of surface groups that are present. The surface morphology of an adsorbent represents a pivotal characteristic in the heavy metal biosorption process.

The biosorption process, which involves the use of biomass to remove metal ions from aqueous solutions, is influenced by various factors that impact its efficacy. The process of reducing and adsorbing Cr (VI) proceeds in a sequence of three distinct stages. The heavy metals removal by microbial-based biomasses and heavy metal biosorption capacity.

The microbial biomasses, as previously mentioned, have been identified as remarkably efficient biosorbents when it comes to removing heavy metal ions. The utilization of microorganisms for bioremediation has proven to be an efficacious technique for alleviating soil and water contamination caused by heavy metals. The microbial-based techniques outlined in this review have the potential to effectively decontaminate both soil and water.

full text: Biosorption is a sustainable technology that uses living or non-living organisms and their derivatives to remove heavy metals from wastewater. Microbial biosorbents have properties that help remediate heavy metal ions from ppm to ppb levels. Biosorption is a metabolic energy process that can be performed by active or dormant microorganisms. The process involves a solid phase (sorbent) and a liquid phase (solvent) with dissolved heavy metal ions. Non-living microorganisms offer advantages like lower sensitivity to toxic wastes and extended storage capabilities. pH is a critical factor affecting biosorption, while temperature has a lesser effect between 20-35°C. Algae, bacteria, and fungi are used in biosorption, with algae having a high sorption capacity and bacteria being adaptable. Fungal cell walls are complex structures that can bind heavy metals. Microbial aggregates are effective for treating wastewater with low heavy metal levels. The removal efficiency of various biomass types for different heavy metals is provided in tables.

full text: The paper discusses the use of microalgae for heavy metal removal, including copper (Cu +2), cadmium (Cd +2), and zinc (Zn +2). Microalgae strains like Chlorella minutissima have shown efficiency in removing these metals. Biochar is also highlighted as an effective adsorbent for heavy metal removal. The mechanisms of bioabsorption and bioaccumulation are discussed, along with examples of successful removal of heavy metals and organic pollutants. The paper emphasizes the environmental and health risks of heavy metal pollution and the potential of bioremediation as a solution.

full text: This review work showed the abilities of microalgae to biologically remove heavy metals from waste water through the process known as ‘Phycoremediation’. Microalgae such as Nannochloropsis oculata, Scenedesmus sp, Scenedesmus obtusus, Gracilaria corticata, Sarcodia suidae and Spirulina sp has been proven to phycoremediate; Pb, Cr, Hg, As, Cd, and Cu respectively. Microalgae are ideally suited for analysing water quality and have been demonstrated to be reliable bio indicators because they have fast reproduction rates, very sensitive responses to chemical changes, eutrophication and pollution, and receive a wide variety of typically specific species. Microalgae are unicellular organisms that consist of both prokaryotic (Cyanophyceae) or eukaryotic (Chlorophyta) organisms and they can grow rapidly in moist environments such as fresh water, waste water, and the marine environment. The ability of microalgae to remove and detoxify heavy metals is the result of several adaptive mechanisms established over centuries of evolution. Microalga contains multifunctional macromolecules such as lipids, proteins and carbohydrates, which offer on its surface different negatively, charged functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, sulfhydryl, sulfate, phosphate, phenol etc. The adsorption mediated by exopolysaccharide is an energy-independent process that occurs via the electrostatic interface between heavy metal cations and the negatively charged functional groups of exopolysaccharides. Phycoremediation of heavy metal is a two-stage process. These techniques use bio-binding or bio-removal methods. In the first stage, heavy metals are adsorbed over the cell surface (biosorption) as shown in Fig. 2. In the second stage, heavy metals are transported across the cell membrane to cytoplasm or other organelles. This helps in the accumulation of the heavy metals inside the algal cells and this process is known as bioaccumulation. Microalgae are efficient and cheap biosorbents due to their high growth rate compared to the terrestrial plants and can complete an entire growing cycle in very few days. Microalgae are beneficial organisms of study as they are key indicators of the toxicity of certain pollutants. The cell walls of microalgae play a key role in heavy metal bioremediation as seen in figure 1. The cell walls typically contain various amounts of starch, glycogen, cellulose, hemicellulose and other polysaccharides that contain specific functional groups which are involved in the binding of metal ions in the biosorption process. Both living and non-living microalgae are used in the removal of heavy metals from sites of contamination, varying in their potentials. Live microalgae, provide limitations such as their dependence on pH and levels of heavy metal contamination; whereas, nonliving algae do not and are accompanied by various practical benefits. A variety of microalgae species find niches in the heavy metal removing environment, just as all organisms must to survive and thrive off their surroundings. Genus Chlorella and Tetraselmis are renowned for their Cd-removing efficiency. Other genuses such as Planothidium, Spirogyra, and Scenedesmus have all shown potential for heavy metal removal in different studies. Phycoremediation is the most reliable and sustainable technology as compared to conventional technologies used for the treatment of waste water. Microalgae have been shown to have the capacity to potentially abate pollution of industrial waste waters by heavy metals.

full text: Biosorption is a widely effective, low-cost, and eco-friendly technology for removing heavy metals from industrial wastewaters. It utilizes naturally available biomaterials and involves physico-chemical processes such as physical adsorption, complexation, ion exchange, oxidation/reduction, and micro-precipitation. The complex composition of biomaterials allows for the binding of heavy metals through various functional groups. In multicomponent systems, competition between metal ions for adsorption sites is significant, and factors like ionic concentration, electronegativity, atomic weight, and temperature affect biosorption capacity. Various adsorption isotherm models, such as Langmuir and Freundlich, are used to analyze and predict biosorption performance. Tables 1, 2, and 3 provide data on the maximum adsorption capacities of different biomaterials for various heavy metals and list top-cited research articles on biosorption.

full text:
A critical review on phycoremediation of pollutants from wastewater—a novel algae-based secondary treatment with the opportunities of production of value-added products.

A large number of microalgal/cyanobacterial strains have been identified that can efficiently remove pollutants from wastewater. The cleaning effect of different species of microalgae/cyanobacteria on wastewater from varied sources along with the value-added products obtained from the algal biomass as observed by researchers during the past few years are reviewed. The efficiency of diverse microalgal/cyanobacterial strains in treatment of wide range of industrial effluents along with municipal wastewater having multi-pollutants has been critically reviewed.

Microalgae are generally monocellular organisms with diameters below 50 micrometer. Different methods of algal cultivation have been developed by researches over time. Waste removal efficiency of microalgal strains vary with the kind of wastewater. Only a few strains (e.g. Chlorella sp. and Scenedesmus sp) can acclimatize well in varied types of wastewaters.

Value-added products are defined as raw products that have been modified to increase their commercial value. Algal biomass can be similarly converted into something useful that has high commercial value. Algal technology has advanced significantly during the past few decades.

Microalgae-bacteria consortia: One of the symbiotic and mutualistic interactions that has benefits for the environment, economics, and energy is the microalgae-bacteria consortium. Algal photobioreactors: Algal photo-bioreactors are open/closed tank systems that are meant to grow certain algae for the removal of target pollutants and/or the synthesis of biofuels. Algal biofilms: Algal biofilms are encapsulated algal populations that have colonised a solid surface and contain an extracellular material matrix.

The tables included in the paper provide specific data on the removal efficiency of different algal strains for various pollutants in different types of wastewater.

full text: Biosorption is a cost-effective and eco-friendly method for removing heavy metals from contaminated water. Biosorbents are derived from various biomasses, including algae, which play a significant role in removing heavy metals. The process is influenced by factors like temperature and pH, and biosorbents offer a promising solution due to their efficacy and environmental friendliness. The paper provides a comprehensive overview of biosorption but does not specifically focus on microalgae-based biosorption of copper, nickel, cobalt, lithium, rare earths, and other heavy or radioactive metals.

full text: Bioaccumulation of metals by microbes -- "bioremoval" -- is a powerful new technology for the concentration, recovery, and removal of toxic heavy metals and radionuclides from waste streams and contaminated environments. Algae are particularly well suited for metal bioremoval. A recent commercial application of bioremoval utilizes inert (dead) immobilized microalgae biomass as ion exchange materials for the removal of heavy metals from industrial waste waters. Also, living microalgal cultures have been used to remove metals from mine effluents. Microbial cells and biomass can bioaccumulate metals and radionuclides by a large variety of mechanisms, both dependent and independent of cell metabolism. Microbial cell walls can act as ion exchange and metal complexation agents. Heavy metals can precipitate and even crystallize on cell surfaces. Metabolically produced hydrogen sulfide or other metabolic products can bioprecipitate heavy metals. Many microbes produce both intra- and extracellular metal complexing agents which could be considered in practical metal removal processes. Bioremoval processes are greatly affected by the microbial species and even strain used, pH, redox potential, temperature, and other conditions under which the microbes are grown. Development of practical applications of bioremoval requires applied research using the particular waste solutions to be treated, or close simulations thereof. From a practical perspective, the selection of the microbial biomass and the process for contacting the microbial biomass with the metal containing solutions are the key issues. Much of the recent commercial R&D has emphasized commercially available, inert, microbial biomass sources as these can be acquired in sufficient quantities at affordable costs. The fundamental research and practical applications of bioaccumulation by microalgae suggests these organisms warrant a high priority in the development of advanced bioremoval processes. The further development of these processes could have significant applications at the $RS and other DOE facilities, as well as environmental protection generally. The practical use of bioaccumulation of metals by microbes is a powerful new technology termed "bioremoval" for the concentration, recovery and removal of toxic heavy metals and radionuclides from waste streams and contaminated environments. Some practical applications of bioaccumulation have already been commercialized in the treatment of industrial waste water and mining effluents. Based on available data, current processes can be greatly improved and new bioremoval technologies could be developed in the near term which could solve many of the environmental problems associated with the release of heavy metals and radionuclides.
